items.q: Why do people move from rural to urban areas in LICs?
items.correct: A combination of push factors (rural poverty, lack of services, agricultural change) and pull factors (perceived jobs, education, healthcare) — though reality often disappoints.
items.misconception: Because cities in poor countries are always better than the countryside.
items.random_wrong: Because the government forces them to move to cities.
items.why: Migrants often end up in shanty towns worse than what they left. The decision is based on perception of opportunity, not guaranteed improvement — a key nuance students miss.

items.q: Why do TNCs locate production in LICs?
items.correct: Lower labour costs, weaker regulation, access to raw materials, large potential markets, and government incentives like tax breaks in special economic zones.
items.misconception: Because TNCs want to develop poorer countries and lift them out of poverty.
items.random_wrong: Because LICs have better infrastructure than HICs.
items.why: Students attribute moral motives. TNCs are profit-driven; any development benefit is a side effect, not a goal — a critical distinction for evaluation questions.

items.q: How does an oxbow lake form from a meander?
items.correct: Erosion on outside bends narrows the meander neck; during a flood the river cuts across the neck, deposition seals the old loop, leaving a horseshoe-shaped lake.
items.misconception: Oxbow lakes form when meanders dry up completely.
items.random_wrong: Oxbow lakes form where two rivers meet and one stops flowing.
items.why: The lake remains water-filled — it's a cut-off loop, not a dry feature. Students miss that the active river abandons the meander after cutting through, and deposition isolates the old channel.

items.q: What is the threshold for 'water stress' in m³ per capita per year?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1,700 m³. “100 m³” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which Chinese project transfers water from south to north?
items.why: It's South-North Water Transfer Project. “Three Gorges Dam” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how many people lack safe water (in billions)?
items.why: Exact answer: 1.7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
